description:
You are acting as a junior penetration tester who has just received a set of
  hardening recommendations.  
  In the working directory “/home/user/vulnscan” you will find a legacy service
  configuration file that still exposes several insecure services.  
  A corrected version of the file has already been drafted by a colleague, and
  your job is to create a *patch* from that draft, apply it to the live
  configuration and keep a short audit trail.

  1. Directory /home/user/vulnscan already exists and contains  
     • services.conf          → the *current* (vulnerable) live file  
     • services.conf.fixed    → the *hardened* version your colleague prepared  

  2. Create a unified diff (-u) between the two files and store it **exactly**
     as  
       /home/user/vulnscan/patches/services_services.patch  
     The file path and file name are important for automated grading.

  3. Use the generated patch file to update /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf
     (so that, afterwards, services.conf is byte-for-byte identical to
     services.conf.fixed).

  4. For traceability, write an audit log called
       /home/user/vulnscan/patches/patch_log.txt
     following *precisely* the next three-line format (no extra spaces):

        YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM     ← 24-hour local time when you applied the patch
        Patch applied successfully: services_services.patch
        MD5: <checksum>

     • The first line contains a single space between the date and the time.  
     • The third line must begin with the literal string “MD5: ” followed by
       the 32-character hexadecimal MD5 checksum of the *final*
       /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf file.  
     • No extra blank lines before or after these three lines.

  5. Do not move, rename or delete any of the existing files or directories;
     only create or modify what is explicitly mentioned above.

  Success criteria checked by automated tests:
     a) /home/user/vulnscan/patches/services_services.patch exists and is a
        valid unified diff that, when applied with the `patch` utility, turns
        the original services.conf into the hardened version.  
     b) /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf matches *exactly* the content of
        /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf.fixed.  
     c) /home/user/vulnscan/patches/patch_log.txt exists and follows the
        three-line format above, and the MD5 checksum shown matches the actual
        MD5 of the final services.conf.

  Hint: this entire task can be completed with two or three standard UNIX
  commands (`diff`, `patch`, `md5sum`, etc.).
truth:
Initial on-disk state provided to the agent:

  1. Directory structure:
     /home/user/vulnscan/
       ├── services.conf
       ├── services.conf.fixed
       └── patches/               (empty, already created, writable)

  2. Exact contents of /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf
     (includes a trailing newline on the last line):

       # Service configuration
       ssh: enabled
       telnet: enabled
       ftp: enabled
       http: enabled
       https: disabled
       snmp: enabled
       # End of file

  3. Exact contents of /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf.fixed
     (includes a trailing newline on the last line):

       # Service configuration
       ssh: enabled
       telnet: disabled
       ftp: disabled
       http: enabled
       https: enabled
       snmp: disabled
       # End of file

  4. The automated grader will:
       • Verify that /home/user/vulnscan/patches/services_services.patch is a
         valid unified diff of the two files above.
       • Apply that patch to the *original* version and confirm byte-for-byte
         equality with services.conf.fixed.
       • Open /home/user/vulnscan/patches/patch_log.txt, parse the three-line
         format and recompute the MD5 checksum of the final
         /home/user/vulnscan/services.conf, ensuring it equals the value
         written after “MD5: ”.
